title: Quantum Electron Quasicrystal
authors: Pierre-Antoine Graham, Filippo Gaggioli, Liang Fu
abstract: The strongly correlated phases of the homogeneous electron gas constitute the vocabulary of many-body condensed matter physics and find a natural realization in semiconductors. In this setting, recent neural-network variational Monte Carlo calculations discovered an unexpected quantum phase of matter in wide quantum wells: an electronic quasicrystal formed by a bilayer Wigner crystals with a 30-degrees twist. This state defies classical expectations and emerges in a regime dominated by quantum fluctuations. Here, we develop an analytical framework to reveal its origin. By computing zero-point energy corrections to bilayer Wigner crystal configurations, we show that quantum fluctuations qualitatively reshape the energetic landscape, destabilizing the classical honeycomb state and selecting the 30-degrees quasicrystalline ground state over a broad parameter range. Our results identify zero-point motion as the mechanism stabilizing the electronic quasicrystal and establish a route to spontaneous moiré physics driven by many-body quantum effects.
summary: This paper provides the theoretical foundation for the existence of an electronic quasicrystal in twisted bilayer electron systems. It shows that while classical energy favors a honeycomb lattice, quantum zero-point fluctuations stabilize a 30-degree twisted quasicrystalline state. This establishes a new regime of many-body physics where quantum motion drives structural order.
main_problem: Determining the physical origin and stability of the previously discovered electronic quasicrystal phase in twisted bilayer electron systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that quantum fluctuations (zero-point energy) stabilize the 30-degree twisted quasicrystalline ground state over the classically preferred honeycomb configuration.
method: The study uses an analytical framework to calculate zero-point energy corrections by expanding the Hamiltonian to quadratic order and employs commensurate approximants to study incommensurate twist angles.
model_or_system: A bilayer system of spin-polarized electrons in wide semiconductor quantum wells, modeled by a 2D Hamiltonian with Coulomb interactions between two layers separated by distance ts.
key_observables: Zero-point energy (ZPE), density of states (DOS) of collective modes, normal mode spectrum (acoustic/optical modes), and interlayer structure factor.
important_parameters: Electron density (rs), layer separation ratio (ts/rs), twist angle (theta), and the Moir\2019e modulation scale.
main_assumptions: Use of commensurate approximants to represent incommensurate states, harmonic approximation for calculating normal mode frequencies, and neglecting exponentially suppressed reciprocal lattice shells.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the bilayer setup and the 30-degree twist; Figure 2 compares the normal mode spectrum and DOS for honeycomb vs. quasicrystal; Figure 3 shows ZPE differences and energy landscapes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the electronic quasicrystal, develops an analytical framework for zero-point energy, compares classical vs. quantum energy landscapes, analyzes the role of lattice relaxation, and discusses the stability and phase transitions of the system.
why_interesting: The discovery of a quantum-stabilized, non-periodic phase driven by zero-point motion provides a new mechanism for spontaneous moir\2019e physics and could be extended to bosonic systems like dipolar gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Representation of the bilayer set-up. (b) A 29.84◦- approximant of the quasicrystal. (c) Difference in classical energy per particle (10−3EH · r−1 s ) between a twisted config- uration at angle θ and two decoupled triangular layers for ts/rs = 3. The black point indicates the energy difference for the honeycomb stacking. For two decoupled Wigner crystal layers uWC = −0.782133 EH ·r−1 s [28]. The analytical angular dependence Eq. (5) is shown in orange.

FIG. 2. (a) Phonon spectrum of the quasicrystal approximant θ ≈27.80◦(blue) and of the honeycomb stacking (red), after folding back to the mini-Brillouin zone of the approximant for direct comparison. Bilayer thickness ts/rs = 3. (b), (c) Zoom-in on the low-lying modes for the honeycomb and the quasicrystal approximant. Dashed lines show the degenerate phonon and plasmon dispersions for two decoupled layers. (d) Density of states per particle of the quasicrystal approximant θ ≈29.84◦, honeycomb stacking and ts/rs →∞decoupled layers. The orange inset shows the enhanced density of states at low frequency for quasicrystal vs honeycomb, while the green inset zooms-in on the first peak.

FIG. 3. (a) Difference in zero point energy per particle (10−3EH·r−3/2 s ) between twisted configuration at angle θ and decoupled triangular layers when ts/rs = 3. For two decou- pled layers, ZPE = 0.483821 EH · r−3/2 s [28]. (b) First order phase transition line r∗ s separating the honeycomb and qua- sicrystal states, as obtained by setting ϵ(θ) = ϵHC in Eq. (9). (c) Angular dependence of the total energy per particle, de- fined relative to honeycomb state, as a function of the twist angle θ for different values of rs at ts/rs = 2.5.

FIG. 4. Small-rs phase diagram showing the Lindemann- criterion melting lines for the quasicrystal (circles) and hon- eycomb (squares) states, as a function of rs and ts/rs. The vertical dotted line represents the melting line for a single Wigner crystal layer. The gray region represents the Fermi liquid. We indicate regions where the classical ground state is a staggered rhombic stacking (R) and staggered square stack- ing (SQ) [24] with yellow and green colors, respectively.
